content.body: Radioactivity is the spontaneous emission of radiation from unstable atomic nuclei. When a nucleus is unstable, it decays by emitting particles or energy to become more stable. This process is random and cannot be sped up or slowed down by external conditions.

The three types of nuclear radiation:
Alpha (α): 2 protons + 2 neutrons (helium nucleus). Charge: +2
Beta (β): high-speed electron from nucleus. Charge: −1
Gamma (γ): high-frequency electromagnetic radiation. No charge, no mass

content.title: Penetrating Power and Ionisation
content.body:
Alpha (α):
• Penetration: stopped by a sheet of paper or few cm of air
• Ionisation: STRONGEST ioniser
• Most dangerous if inhaled/ingested (inside body)

Beta (β):
• Penetration: stopped by a few mm of aluminium
• Ionisation: moderate

Gamma (γ):
• Penetration: needs several cm of lead or metres of concrete
• Ionisation: weakest ioniser
• Most dangerous externally (penetrates the body)

Nuclear Equations:
Alpha decay: mass number decreases by 4, atomic number decreases by 2
²³⁸₉₂U → ²³⁴₉₀Th + ⁴₂He

Beta decay: atomic number increases by 1, mass number unchanged
¹⁴₆C → ¹⁴₇N + ⁰₋₁e

Half-Life (t₁/₂): time for half of the radioactive nuclei in a sample to decay.

After 1 half-life: N/2 remain
After 2 half-lives: N/4 remain
After 3 half-lives: N/8 remain
After n half-lives: N/2ⁿ remain

content.title: Half-Life Calculation
content.body:
A sample has 800 atoms. Half-life = 3 days. How many remain after 9 days?
9 days = 3 half-lives
After 3 half-lives: 800/2³ = 800/8 = 100 atoms remain

questions.question: Which radiation has the greatest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma radiation penetrates furthest — it requires lead or thick concrete to stop it.

questions.question: An alpha particle consists of:
questions.explanation: An alpha particle is a helium-4 nucleus: 2 protons and 2 neutrons.

questions.question: What stops alpha radiation?
options: Paper or a few cm of air
questions.answer: Paper or a few cm of air
questions.explanation: Alpha particles are heavy and slow — stopped by paper or a few centimetres of air.

questions.question: In alpha decay, the mass number:
questions.explanation: An alpha particle has mass number 4. When emitted, mass number of parent decreases by 4.

questions.question: Half-life is the time for:
options: The sample to lose half its mass
Half the radiation to be emitted
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time for half of the radioactive atoms in a sample to decay.

questions.question: A sample of 1600 atoms. After 2 half-lives, how many remain?
questions.explanation: After 2 half-lives: 1600/2² = 1600/4 = 400 atoms.

questions.question: Beta radiation is:
questions.explanation: Beta particles are high-speed electrons emitted from the nucleus during beta decay.

questions.question: Gamma radiation has:
questions.explanation: Gamma radiation is electromagnetic — it has no mass and no charge.

questions.question: Which radiation is most ionising?
questions.explanation: Alpha particles are the strongest ionisers because they are large, slow, and highly charged.

questions.question: Half-life = 5 years. After 15 years, what fraction of the sample remains?
questions.explanation: 15 years = 3 half-lives. Fraction remaining = (1/2)³ = 1/8.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: While radioactive materials are dangerous, they have many important applications in medicine, industry, and energy production. Understanding both uses and safety measures is essential.

content.title: Medical Uses
content.body:
Cancer treatment (radiotherapy): gamma rays from Cobalt-60 kill cancer cells
Diagnosis (tracers): radioactive iodine-131 accumulates in thyroid — scanned to detect problems
Sterilisation: gamma rays sterilise medical equipment without heat
PET scans: positron emitters trace metabolic activity in the brain

content.title: Industrial Uses
content.body:
Thickness gauges: beta radiation measures paper/metal sheet thickness
Smoke detectors: alpha source (Americium-241) ionises air — smoke breaks current, triggering alarm
Radiocarbon dating: Carbon-14 (half-life 5730 years) used to date ancient organic material
Nuclear power stations: controlled fission of Uranium-235 produces enormous amounts of electricity

Dangers of radiation:
• Ionisation damages DNA, potentially causing mutations and cancer
• High doses cause radiation sickness (nausea, hair loss, organ failure)
• Long-term low-dose exposure increases cancer risk
• Alpha particles most dangerous internally (inside body)

Safety precautions:
• Wear protective clothing and dosimeter badges
• Handle sources with long-handled tongs (never with bare hands)
• Store radioactive materials in lead-lined containers
• Minimise time of exposure
• Maximise distance from source (intensity drops with square of distance)
• Use lead/concrete shielding

questions.question: Radiocarbon dating uses Carbon-14 because:
options: It has a very short half-life
It has a half-life of ~5730 years (useful timescale)
questions.answer: It has a half-life of ~5730 years (useful timescale)
questions.explanation: Carbon-14's half-life of ~5730 years is ideal for dating objects thousands of years old.

questions.question: How do smoke detectors use radioactivity?
options: Alpha source ionises air; smoke stops ionisation, triggering alarm
Gamma rays react with smoke chemically
questions.answer: Alpha source ionises air; smoke stops ionisation, triggering alarm
questions.explanation: An alpha source (Americium-241) ionises air allowing current to flow. Smoke absorbs alpha particles, breaking the current and triggering the alarm.

questions.question: Why is gamma radiation used to sterilise medical equipment?
options: It penetrates packaging and kills bacteria without heat
questions.answer: It penetrates packaging and kills bacteria without heat
questions.explanation: Gamma radiation penetrates sealed packaging and kills microorganisms without needing to open or heat the equipment.

questions.question: Why should radioactive sources be handled with tongs?
options: They are too heavy to pick up
To reduce exposure by maximising distance
Tongs are required by law only
questions.answer: To reduce exposure by maximising distance
questions.explanation: Greater distance from the source reduces radiation exposure.

questions.question: What type of radiation is used in cancer radiotherapy?
questions.explanation: Gamma radiation from Cobalt-60 is used to target and kill cancer cells in radiotherapy.

questions.question: Why is alpha radiation most dangerous inside the body?
options: It has the most penetrating power
It causes the most ionisation and damage to nearby cells
It converts to gamma inside the body
questions.answer: It causes the most ionisation and damage to nearby cells
questions.explanation: Alpha particles are the strongest ionisers. Inside the body, they are close to cells and cause extensive DNA damage.

questions.question: Radioactive tracers in medicine are usually:
options: Short half-life gamma/beta emitters that leave the body quickly
questions.answer: Short half-life gamma/beta emitters that leave the body quickly
questions.explanation: Medical tracers must emit detectable radiation (gamma/beta) and have short half-lives so they decay quickly and don't remain harmful in the body.

questions.question: Nuclear power stations generate electricity through:
questions.explanation: Nuclear power stations split (fission) heavy nuclei like Uranium-235, releasing enormous heat energy to generate electricity.

questions.question: What material is used to store radioactive sources safely?
questions.explanation: Lead is dense and absorbs radiation effectively, making it ideal for shielding and storage.

questions.question: Radiation causes cancer by:
questions.explanation: Ionising radiation damages DNA strands. If the damage causes mutations in cell division genes, this can lead to cancer.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body:
Half-life (t½): time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
N = N₀ (1/2)^(t/t½)
N₀ = initial number of atoms, N = remaining atoms after time t

Activity A = λN
λ = decay constant = 0.693/t½
A decreases by half every half-life

content.title: Half-life Calculation
content.body:
A sample has 8000 radioactive atoms. t½ = 5 years.
After 5 years: 4000 atoms
After 10 years: 2000 atoms
After 15 years: 1000 atoms
After 20 years: 500 atoms
(Fraction remaining after 4 half-lives = (1/2)⁴ = 1/16)

content.title: Applications of Half-life
content.body:
Carbon-14 dating: t½ = 5730 years → dates organic material up to ~50,000 years old
Uranium-238: t½ = 4.5 billion years → dates rocks
Technetium-99m: t½ = 6 hours → safe medical tracer
Cobalt-60: t½ = 5.3 years → cancer treatment radiation

Nuclear equations:
α decay: ²³⁸₉₂U → ²³⁴₉₀Th + ⁴₂He
β⁻ decay: ¹⁴₆C → ¹⁴₇N + ⁰₋₁e
γ emission: no change in mass or atomic number
Conservation: mass numbers and atomic numbers must balance

questions.question: Half-life is...
options: Time for all atoms to decay
Time for half the radioactive atoms to decay
Time for one atom to decay
questions.answer: Time for half the radioactive atoms to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life (t½) is the time taken for exactly half the radioactive nuclei in a sample to decay.

questions.question: A sample starts with 1600 atoms. After 3 half-lives remain...
questions.explanation: After 3 half-lives: 1600 × (1/2)³ = 1600/8 = 200 atoms

questions.question: Carbon-14 dating is used to...
questions.answer: Date organic materials up to ~50,000 years old
questions.explanation: C-14 (t½=5730 years) is in all living organisms. After death, C-14 decays. Measuring ratio to C-12 gives age.

questions.question: Why is Tc-99m ideal for medical imaging?
options: Short t½=6h (safe dose) and emits γ rays for detection
questions.answer: Short t½=6h (safe dose) and emits γ rays for detection
questions.explanation: Medical tracers need: short t½ (low radiation dose), γ emission (detected outside body), right chemistry to target organ.

questions.question: In α decay, the nucleus loses...
questions.answer: 2 protons and 2 neutrons (mass number −4, atomic number −2)
questions.explanation: Alpha particle = helium nucleus = 2p + 2n. Mass number decreases by 4, atomic number by 2.

questions.question: In β⁻ decay, a neutron becomes a proton by emitting...
questions.explanation: In β⁻ decay: neutron → proton + electron + antineutrino. Atomic number increases by 1, mass unchanged.

questions.question: Gamma radiation accompanies alpha or beta decay. What changes?
questions.explanation: Gamma emission just releases excess energy from an excited nucleus — no change in mass or atomic number.

questions.question: Activity A = λN means activity is proportional to...
questions.explanation: Activity (decays/second) = λ × N. More atoms remaining → more decays per second.

questions.question: Uranium-238 (t½=4.5 billion years) is used to...
options: Date the age of ancient rocks and Earth
questions.answer: Date the age of ancient rocks and Earth
questions.explanation: Very long half-life → still decaying slowly after billions of years → compare U-238 to its decay product Pb-206 to date rocks.

questions.question: A nuclear equation must conserve...
options: Mass number and atomic number on both sides
questions.answer: Mass number and atomic number on both sides
questions.explanation: In nuclear equations, both the total mass number (top) and total atomic number (bottom) must balance on each side.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Radioactivity. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Radioactivity concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of physics.
Core Theory

This section of Radioactivity builds on foundational knowledge. In physics, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 4.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Radioactivity. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 4.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Radioactivity. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Radioactivity include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Radioactivity. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.

Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.
